yes: Ah, good to see another serviceman. Stand easy.
Glad to see you're still in uniform. Maintaining standards even now.
no: Civilian, this is a restricted military area.
I say, are you authorized to be here?
Good day. I'm Lieutenant Parker. And you are?

responses.text: I'll leave you to your duties.

dynamic_line: Lieutenant Parker, Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, class of '21.
Captain Williams, formerly attached to 16 Air Assault Brigade.
Major Thompson, 42 Commando, Royal Marines.
Lieutenant Davies, 1st Battalion, Parachute Regiment.

responses.text: How did you end up here?

dynamic_line: Dire, to put it mildly. We've lost contact with high command and most of our forces are scattered.
We're maintaining a defensive perimeter here while gathering intelligence on the situation. Resources are limited.
Attempting to establish contact with other surviving military units. So far, communications have been spotty at best.
Frankly, it's a complete shambles. Chain of command has collapsed, and we're operating on outdated contingency plans.
I'm the senior officer in this immediate area, yes. Though that's not saying much these days.
Technically, yes. Though the traditional command structure has rather broken down.
I'm coordinating our local efforts. There may be higher-ranking officers elsewhere, if they've survived.
I've assumed command of the surviving personnel in this sector, in accordance with protocol.

responses.text: How many personnel do you have?

dynamic_line: A handful of infantry, plus some support staff. We've lost too many good people.
Not enough, I'm afraid. We started with a full platoon, now we're down to squad strength.
Our numbers fluctuate. We've been taking in stragglers from other units when we find them.
That information is need-to-know. Suffice to say, we're undermanned for our objectives.
I was leading a training exercise when everything went pear-shaped. We lost communications, then the attacks started.
I was stationed at the embassy when the crisis began. Had to fight our way out when the local population... changed.
Was on a NATO exchange program. Found myself cut off from British forces when it all went to hell.
I was coordinating with American forces when we lost contact with command. Been trying to maintain order ever since.
Indeed. But one carries on, doesn't one? The job remains the same: protect civilians, maintain order.
We're trained to adapt and overcome. Though I must say, the current situation rather exceeds the scenarios covered at Sandhurst.
Thank you for your concern. It's been... challenging. Lost a lot of good people.
Stiff upper lip and all that. We do what we must.
Primary objective is to secure this location and gather intelligence on the wider situation.
We're attempting to establish contact with any remaining command structure and assist civilians where possible.
Currently focused on survival and reconnaissance. We need to understand what we're dealing with before formulating a broader strategy.
Immediate goal is to secure essential supplies and establish a defensible position. Long-term planning is... complicated.

responses.text: Do you need any assistance with that?

dynamic_line: Actually, yes. We're desperately short on medical supplies. If you could locate some, it would be tremendously helpful.
We need to establish communications with other surviving units. There's supposed to be a military radio in a supply cache nearby.
Our reconnaissance is limited by manpower. If you could scout the surrounding area and report back, that would be valuable intelligence.
We've lost contact with a patrol that was sent to secure supplies. If you could determine their fate, I'd be grateful.

responses.text: I'll see what I can do.

dynamic_line.yes: Excellent. Your assistance is greatly appreciated. Report back when you have something to share.
dynamic_line.no: I'm not sure what you're referring to.

dynamic_line.yes: I understand. These are difficult times. Perhaps you'll reconsider when circumstances allow.
dynamic_line.no: I'm not sure what you're referring to.
